1: The Philistines fight Israel; the men of Israel flee before the Philistines and fall slain on Mount Gilboa. The Battle of Gilboa (modern Jebel Fuqūʿa, the ridge on the south side of the Jezreel Valley) was one of the most decisive military disasters in Israelite history. The Jezreel Valley provided the flat terrain where Philistine chariot forces had overwhelming tactical advantage against Israelite infantry on foot. The Israelite position on Gilboa — elevated but exposed and outnumbered — offered no favorable defensive options against a coordinated assault.
2: The Philistines overtake Saul and his sons; Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua die. Jonathan's death here — in the same battle David was prevented from fighting in (1 Samuel 29) — is the covenant friend's end. Jonathan, who had consistently acknowledged David's future kingship, dies protecting his father's doomed kingdom. The narrative has nothing more to say about Jonathan; his covenant with David remains enacted in 2 Sam 9's treatment of Mephibosheth.
3: The battle goes hard against Saul. The archers find him and he is critically wounded. The Philistine archers — the ranged-weapons specialists who could engage from outside hand-combat range — are what ultimately breaks Saul's position. Whether Saul's wounds are from arrows in flight or from archers who had closed to short range, the effect is that the king is incapacitated before a decisive hand-to-hand encounter.
4: Saul asks his armor-bearer to run him through — 'lest these uncircumcised come and abuse me.' The concern for posthumous bodily violation was widespread in the ancient world: desecrating the enemy king's body was a way of continuing the military humiliation beyond death. Saul's request is the honor-death of a warrior who will not allow his enemies the satisfaction of killing and desecrating him. The armor-bearer is unwilling to strike YHWH's anointed — even at his own king's request — and Saul falls on his own sword.
5: The armor-bearer, seeing Saul dead, also falls on his sword and dies with him. The death of the armor-bearer following his king is the ANE loyalty custom of the faithful retainer — documented in Mesopotamian burial practices and in the practice of servants being entombed with their masters. The armor-bearer's refusal to kill Saul was covenant reverence; his suicide at Saul's death was covenant loyalty of a different kind.
6: Saul and his three sons and his armor-bearer and all his men die together that day. The summary is comprehensive: the royal household and its immediate military support are eliminated in a single engagement. This is the fulfillment of Samuel's Endor oracle (1 Samuel 28:19: 'tomorrow you and your sons will be with me'). The timing — 'that day' — confirms the oracle's precision.
7: The men of Israel on the other side of the valley and beyond the Jordan see that Israel's men fled and Saul and his sons are dead — they abandon the cities and flee. The Philistines come and live in them. The collapse of the Israelite military position triggers a regional population flight — settlements that had been under Israelite administration become Philistine-occupied. The scale of the territorial loss sets the context for David's kingship challenge: he inherits a kingdom with a significant Philistine presence in the central valley.
8: The next day, the Philistines come to strip the dead and find Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa. Stripping the battlefield dead was standard post-battle practice across the ancient Near East — equipment, weapons, jewelry, and clothing were all valuable military spoil. The specific finding of the royal bodies transformed what would have been routine plunder into a significant political-religious statement.
9: They cut off Saul's head and strip his armor, sending word throughout the land of the Philistines and to the temples of their idols. Saul's head is displayed in the temple of Dagon (1 Chronicles 10:10); his armor is put in the temple of Ashtaroth. The division of the trophy between two temples reflects the Philistine polytheistic practice of distributing victory honors across the pantheon. Beth-shan (modern Tell el-Husn, adjacent to Tell Beth She'an) was a major temple city with excavated temples consistent with this period.
10: They fasten his body to the wall of Beth-shan. Archaeological excavations at Tell Beth She'an have uncovered massive temple complexes from the Iron Age I period — the site was a major Egyptian administrative center in the Late Bronze Age and continued as a significant city in the early Iron Age. The wall-display of Saul's body at Beth-shan is both a military trophy and a religious statement: YHWH's anointed is displayed at a Philistine religious center as evidence of Philistine divine victory over the God of Israel.
11: The inhabitants of Jabesh-Gilead hear what the Philistines did to Saul. Jabesh-Gilead's response is immediate and courageous. The relationship between Jabesh-Gilead and Saul began in 1 Samuel 11 where Saul's Spirit-empowered rescue of Jabesh-Gilead from the Ammonite siege was his first royal act. The city had been saved by Saul; now they repay the debt by recovering his body at significant personal risk — Beth-shan was in Philistine-controlled territory.
12: All the valiant men of Jabesh-Gilead rise and travel through the night, take the bodies of Saul and his sons from the wall of Beth-shan, and bring them to Jabesh. The distance from Jabesh-Gilead (Tell Abu Kharaz in Transjordan) to Beth-shan is approximately 20-25 km — a significant night march across the Jordan and through hostile territory. The 'valiant men' (ʾanšê ḥayil) who undertake this mission risk their lives out of covenant loyalty to the king who delivered them.
13: They burn the bodies at Jabesh and bury the bones under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh, fasting for seven days. The burning of the bodies was unusual in Israelite practice — normally bodies were buried intact. The burning may have been necessitated by the advanced decomposition of bodies exposed on a wall, or it may reflect a practice associated with royal cremation. The tamarisk tree (ʾešel) is the same species under which Saul held court at Gibeah (1 Samuel 22:6) and under which he was eventually reburied (1 Chronicles 10:12). The seven-day fast is the full mourning period, honoring the king who delivered Jabesh-Gilead in his first act of kingship.
